unit.name: GPT-3, in-context learning, scaling laws, and prompting
unit.description: Explains how very large decoder-only models exhibit in-context learning, discusses compute/data scaling tradeoffs, and introduces chain-of-thought prompting along with broader capability and safety questions.
unit.summary: The final segment centers on GPT-3, a much larger decoder-only model that could sometimes perform new tasks without parameter updates, simply from examples placed in the prompt [ts=3995s] [ts=4052s]. This behavior is named in-context learning: the model infers a pattern from demonstrations in the current input—translation, addition, typo correction—and continues it without explicit fine-tuning [ts=4052s] [ts=4102s]. The lecture stresses that the mechanism is still poorly understood: some behavior may come from matching patterns seen during pretraining, while some appears to reflect nontrivial adaptation to the presented context [ts=4141s] [ts=4230s]. It then turns to scaling, noting that larger models trained on more data generally improved, but that compute must be allocated wisely; Chinchilla is cited as evidence that GPT-3 was over-parameterized relative to the amount of training data and that smaller models trained on more tokens can do better [ts=4234s] [ts=4290s]. Finally, the segment introduces chain-of-thought prompting as giving worked intermediate steps in the prompt, which often improves reasoning by letting the model generate a scratchpad, while also emphasizing open concerns about emergent behavior, bias, programming use, and continued domain-adaptive pretraining such as the "Don't Stop Pretraining" idea [ts=4310s] [ts=4634s].

key_points.text: GPT-3 can sometimes solve tasks from prompt examples alone, without gradient-based fine-tuning.
In-context learning means inferring and continuing a task pattern from demonstrations within the current input.
Whether in-context learning is memorized pattern matching or more general task learning remains an open research question.
Scaling requires balancing parameter count against training tokens; Chinchilla improved by using fewer parameters and more data.
Chain-of-thought prompting provides intermediate reasoning steps in the prompt and often improves answer accuracy.
Pretraining yields broad capabilities but also biases and hard-to-predict failures.
A practical extension is to continue pretraining on unlabeled in-domain text before final supervised fine-tuning.
